full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> At the end of April, I was invited to lead a retreat<|6.44|> <|6.44|> for the Daughters of the King at Cathedral Ridge.<|10.24|> <|11.14|> And it was a wonderful weekend of prayer and singing<|15.22|> <|15.22|> and fellowship and learning,<|17.22|> <|17.92|> learning about God and learning about each other.<|20.62|> <|21.90|> And the theme of the weekend was<|24.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I did not make it up.<|2.06|> <|2.72|> The basis for all of my teaching and all of our discussion was from Psalm 46.<|8.60|> <|9.82|> Be still and know that I am God.<|12.34|> <|13.36|> We know I didn't make this up because I can't offer much on the be still part.<|19.26|> <|20.52|> Stillness is not my strong suit.<|22.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So we spent a lot of time exploring how we know God.<|7.04|> <|8.26|> Scripture? Yes.<|10.04|> <|10.78|> Teaching? Yes.<|12.70|> <|13.98|> Creation? Yes.<|16.34|> <|17.20|> Especially here in Colorado.<|18.86|> <|19.96|> Prayer? Yes.<|21.88|> <|22.38|> All of these are assuredly ways of knowing God.<|28.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The daughters were in small groups discussing questions and conversation prompts that I had given them.<|8.24|> <|9.12|> And as I walked around and eavesdropped with their permission, I heard wonderful and rich conversation.<|16.56|> <|17.06|> And I said,<|17.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But I realized that a lot of what I was hearing was them sharing what they had observed about God.<|10.10|> <|10.76|> It was how they knew God from a bit of a distance and how they saw God moving in the lives of others.<|20.08|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But to really, really know someone, we must allow ourselves to experience them, right?<|11.88|> <|11.88|> So we talked about how we know people.<|16.54|> <|16.54|> Someone can tell you all about a person, how wonderful and warm and kind they are, but<|24.06|> <|24.06|> you don't know them.<|26.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You certainly can't say you have a relationship with them.<|5.76|> <|5.76|> Relationships are not second hand.<|7.84|> <|7.84|> I mean, I can read all about celebrities in People magazine and follow them on Instagram,<|14.88|> <|14.88|> but that will never make them my friend.<|18.50|> <|18.50|> We know things about them, but we do not know them.<|24.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So, I think thinking about how we know people helps us think about how we know God.<|9.22|> <|10.26|> Fellowship, conversation, time spent together, interaction.<|14.32|> <|14.96|> It can be a bit scary to speak of our experiences of God and the ways that we have seen and felt God show up in our lives today.<|29.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> especially if God has turned our lives upside down.<|5.50|> <|6.82|> So then there was a shift in the discussion,<|9.32|> <|9.88|> a trust to share some of how we had personally experienced God<|16.60|> <|16.60|> through healings,<|19.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Through death, through miraculous changes in circumstance, unmerited forgiveness,<|8.86|> <|10.04|> or experiencing that peace that passes all understanding even in the midst of chaos.<|17.62|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> These are all ways of experiencing and knowing because they are all personal.<|8.20|> <|8.20|> And these can be scary stories to share.<|13.26|> <|13.26|> They make us feel vulnerable.<|15.26|> <|15.26|> And Lord knows, as Episcopalians, we like to think of ourselves as intellectual and rational<|21.04|> <|21.04|> Christians.<|23.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So I've been pondering this all week as I've wrestled with today's gospel lesson.<|6.88|> <|9.00|> To back up a few verses and add context, which is always a good thing in Scripture, I'll<|16.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus sets out on the lake with his disciples, and he falls asleep while they are sailing.<|7.40|> <|8.24|> A storm comes up, and the boat takes on water.<|11.86|> <|13.26|> The disciples awaken the sleeping Jesus in a panic, and he's asleep.<|17.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They declare that they are perishing, and Jesus gets up and rebuked the wind and the raging waves, and they ceased, and there was calm.<|15.92|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Luke goes on to say that this experience of Jesus left the disciples terrified and amazed,<|10.28|> <|10.28|> and they said to one another,<|12.32|> <|12.52|> Who then is this that He commands even the winds and the water, and they obey Him?<|20.22|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And this is the exact point where we enter today's lesson.<|4.84|> <|5.72|> They're coming ashore after this experience, and the disciples are asking each other,<|12.66|> <|13.06|> who is Jesus after seeing Him tame nature itself?<|19.86|> <|19.86|> Amen.<|21.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When Sampertaro was with us during Lent, he talked of the cinematic nature of Luke's gospel,<|8.34|> <|8.34|> the special effects and the heightened drama compared to some of the other gospels.<|14.48|> <|15.10|> I think this is a great example of that.<|19.04|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Fresh off a tamed tempest, Jesus steps onto the shore, his only time in Gentile territory<|9.60|> <|9.60|> in this gospel.<|10.54|> <|10.76|> So those of you confused about the pigs, there you have it.<|13.64|> <|14.56|> To a scene straight out of a movie, a demon-possessed man who has superhuman strength, enabling<|23.66|> <|23.66|> him to break free from shackles and chains.<|27.86|> <|28.20|> Amen.<|28.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> However, he is only free to wander among the unclean tombs of the dead and the unclean herds of swine,<|10.90|> <|11.62|> wearing nothing and possessed by not just one demon, but according to his name, Legion.<|20.48|> <|21.58|> Legion.<|22.20|> <|22.20|> Legion.<|23.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Now, a legion was a Roman military designation for 6,000 troops, 6,000 hostile occupiers.<|14.04|> <|14.04|> And even if this name is Hollywood-esque hyperbole, it makes a point, and this would not have<|21.24|> <|21.24|> been lost on Luke's original audience.<|25.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> My friends, I think there is added tragedy in this story<|8.24|> <|8.24|> that the man's affliction has robbed him of his name,<|14.24|> <|14.24|> of his very identity.<|16.58|> <|16.58|> I'm reminded of my time with the ill who would prefer to say,<|24.12|> <|24.12|> "I am a person living with cancer."<|28.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> or our attempt to humanize some of our neighbors as people experiencing homelessness,<|8.48|> <|9.56|> or the movement to say their name when people have died by unjust violence,<|17.20|> <|17.20|> or,<|17.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> so that humans are not diminished to statistics or labels or categories, addictions or afflictions.<|11.84|> <|12.76|> We should always try to restore humanity.<|17.40|> <|18.40|> We are always more than what ails us.<|21.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But Legion has lost his identity to his affliction, and Jesus restores it, because that is what Jesus does.<|12.60|> <|13.66|> The demons are driven out of the man into a herd of swine that then plunge themselves into the lake.<|22.06|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This is nothing less than a cosmic spiritual battle with tombs and abyss, demons, and a miraculous healing.<|10.72|> <|11.84|> It is Hollywood worthy indeed.<|14.58|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> need.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.90|> Luke doesn't share with us how the disciples responded to all of this.<|6.22|> <|6.58|> They're not mentioned at all.<|8.14|> <|9.02|> But we do know how the man responds,<|11.82|> <|12.82|> and the city and country folk who hear from the swine herds.<|16.88|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Luke tells us, "When the swine herds saw what had happened, they ran off and told in the<|6.44|> <|6.44|> city and in the country.<|9.04|> <|9.04|> And we're not exactly sure what they were told.<|13.08|> <|13.08|> Was it a complaint from the swine herds that their livelihood had just been destroyed?"<|19.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Was it that the outcast was now truly free?<|4.66|> <|5.76|> Was it that there was a sorcerer in their midst or that God was truly present in this man, Jesus?<|14.50|> <|15.64|> We don't know how they shared their experience.<|20.44|> <|20.44|> We don't know how they shared their experience.<|20.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> but it brought people running.<|2.36|> <|3.14|> Both the city and the country folk,<|5.42|> <|5.50|> they saw for themselves that the possessed man<|8.46|> <|8.46|> was now clean, clothed, and calm,<|12.64|> <|14.02|> sitting at Jesus' feet.<|15.54|> <|17.04|> And their response to this experience was,<|20.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They were afraid.<|2.04|> <|3.12|> They were afraid, and they asked Jesus to leave.<|7.70|> <|10.54|> I love that godly play question.<|14.16|> <|15.06|> I wonder where you are in this story.<|18.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and what part of this story is about you.<|4.08|> <|5.34|> And that may seem like a ridiculous question in the face of this wild story,<|10.16|> <|10.16|> but stick with me.<|11.64|> <|13.68|> Are you the man who has lost his name<|17.94|> <|17.94|> and is identified only by his affliction?<|22.62|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.08|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Are you the man who has been returned to wholeness, free to inhabit your full humanity?<|9.36|> <|11.50|> Are you the people who are so afraid of having their lives changed that they ask Jesus to leave?<|22.88|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Are you the man who cannot help but proclaim after your experience of God how much Jesus<|12.86|> <|12.86|> has done for you?<|14.06|> <|16.06|> Notice that several of these are the same individual, but they represent different responses.<|22.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I would venture that as fantastic as this story is, we are all present in all parts of this story at one time or another in our lives.<|14.20|> <|16.64|> Like my time with the daughters, I ask you, how do you know God?<|25.84|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What is your personal experience of the divine?<|6.06|> <|7.18|> Where has God met you and changed your life, maybe even restored your name?<|14.46|> <|16.28|> And how have you shared this knowing, this experience?<|21.18|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thanks.<|1.22|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I wonder where you are in this story<|5.80|> <|5.80|> and what part of this story is about you.<|10.24|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|6.24|><|endoftext|>
